O-01.name: Supply chain concentration & disruption

supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.source: erp_supply_chain.csv — TSMC critical-path supplier (18-week lead time)
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.description: Inventory cover weeks for the critical-path single-source supplier (TSMC, 18-week lead time). Amber threshold 8w = TSMC target_inventory_weeks from ERP. Breach threshold 4w = minimum safe operating level (~22% of TSMC lead time). Current 5.2w = amber: 2.8 weeks below target, 12.8 weeks below TSMC lead time — a TSMC disruption today results in production stop in 5.2 weeks with no substitution path.
supplier_recovery_runway_weeks.threshold_rationale: TSMC 18-week lead time benchmark: amber at target inventory (8w), breach at 22% of lead time (4w)

O-01.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK ASSESSMENT — 2026-05-28

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — BREACH (3 KRIs in breach, 1 amber): ESCALATION REQUIRED

The supply chain domain presents the most acute near-term business continuity threat in this assessment cycle. Single-source concentration stands at 62.6% of $12.6B total spend, breaching the 50% threshold and sitting 12.6 percentage points above it. The three single-source suppliers — TSMC ($4.2B, Semiconductors-CPU), Foxconn ($2.1B, Manufacturing-Assembly), and Quanta ($1.6B, ODM-Laptops) — account for $7.9B of spend with no alternative sourcing pathway. The supply chain VaR model places 95th-percentile loss exposure at $2.634B and revenue at risk at $9.1B against a tail disruption scenario.

The inventory position is critical. Minimum cover across the portfolio is 3.9 weeks, breaching the 4-week floor, and the weakest position belongs to Quanta at 3.9 weeks against a 6-week lead time — meaning any Quanta disruption today results in an immediate gap with no buffer. TSMC, the highest-spend single-source supplier, carries 5.2 weeks of cover, but its 18-week lead time means that once stock is consumed, recovery extends to 5 months per the stress model. The organisation currently has no ability to rebuild CPU semiconductor inventory within any commercially meaningful timeframe following a TSMC disruption event.

The Quanta situation triggers the escalation protocol: financial_health score of 61 falls below the 65 distress threshold, and inventory cover sits at exactly 3.9 weeks — below the 4-week breach floor. This is the single-source, low-health, low-inventory combination that represents maximum concentration risk. Quanta's OTD rate of 88.7% is the lowest of the three single-source suppliers, and its rejection rate of 1.2% is the highest. Yangtze Memory [SUP008], a multi-source supplier, also carries a distress score of 55 — the lowest financial health recorded across the supplier base — though multi-source status provides partial mitigation.

Geographic concentration amplifies all of the above. Taiwan accounts for 53.5% of spend (hosting TSMC and Quanta), China for 21.8% (Foxconn), and South Korea for 21.3%. A Taiwan-specific disruption — geopolitical escalation, major seismic event, or TSMC operational failure — would simultaneously affect $4.2B of CPU semiconductor spend and $1.6B of ODM-Laptop spend, covering 46% of total spend in a single geographic event. The stress model quantifies dual-sourcing savings at $382M and inventory buffer savings at $305M — these represent the cost of not acting on known concentration risk, not savings already realised.

BCM verdict: The organisation cannot sustain current production rates beyond 3.9 weeks in a severe supply disruption, and cannot recover within less than 5 months in a Taiwan-concentrated scenario. Revenue exposure is $9.1B. The 24% demand shock probability flagged in the model, carrying a 15% impact, adds a secondary vector to a supply chain already operating with no buffer.

---

O-02 CYBER AND IT — BREACH (6 KRIs in breach, 5 amber): ESCALATION REQUIRED

The cyber domain shows a pattern of compounding control failures rather than a single acute incident. Six metrics are in breach simultaneously, and the combination of a long detection window, slow response, unpatched estate, and zero supplier assessment creates a threat environment where an active adversary could operate undetected and uncontained for an extended period.

MTTD of 11 days breaches the 10-day threshold and means a threat actor has, on average, 11 days of undetected access before any detection signal is raised. When combined with an MTTR of 18 days, the total detection-to-remediation window is approximately 29 days. This is the most consequential control failure in this domain: modern ransomware and data exfiltration campaigns can achieve full compromise objectives within 72 hours of initial access. A 29-day window is operationally indefensible.

Patch compliance at 71% breaches the 80% floor, leaving 29% of the estate unpatched. Eight critical vulnerabilities have been open for more than 30 days, below the breach threshold of 10 but trending in the wrong direction given the detection impairment. Privileged access has not been reviewed for 45 days, creating an uncontrolled lateral movement and insider threat vector. MFA coverage at 87% leaves 13% of access points without a second authentication factor — a meaningful attack surface given the 2.3% phishing attempt rate.

The most structurally concerning finding is supplier cyber resilience assessment at 0%. Not a single supplier has been assessed. Against a backdrop of 4 security incidents month-to-date and a 38% third-party vendor assessment gap, this meets the escalation trigger: zero assessment coverage with active security incidents in the current period. The supply chain interconnection (see O-01 + O-02 flag) is direct: TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta have unassessed cyber postures while collectively representing $7.9B of single-source spend and serving as production-critical dependencies.

The IT RTO of 6 hours breaches the 4-hour amber threshold. An OMS outage beyond 6 hours would interrupt order processing and fulfilment, with direct revenue recognition impact. The combination of breach-level RTO, unpatched estate, and 29-day detection-to-response window means that a significant incident could breach the RTO objective before the organisation is aware an incident has occurred.

BCM verdict: The current cyber control posture cannot reliably detect, contain, or recover from a significant incident within declared operational tolerances. Revenue continuity through the OMS depends on a system with an unmet recovery objective. The EBITDA cost of a major cyber incident — incident response, regulatory notification, potential ransom, reputational impact — is not quantifiable from available data, but the structural control gaps are severe and unmitigated.

---

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — AMBER (3 KRIs amber, 0 breach): NO ESCALATION

The product quality domain is the most stable across this assessment cycle, with no safety incidents, no regulatory notices, and a field failure rate of 0.019% — well within the 0.05% amber threshold and far from the 0.10% breach level. First-pass yield of 97.2% is a healthy signal. There are no grounds for escalation under the zero-tolerance safety incident trigger.

However, two amber signals warrant focused attention and are trending in the wrong direction. Recall readiness score of 72 sits only 2 points above the breach threshold of 70, and the last recall simulation was conducted 8 months ago — an unacceptably long gap for a domain with zero tolerance for safety failures. If a safety event were to emerge without a rehearsed, validated recall process, the organisation's ability to execute a compliant, efficient product withdrawal would be untested. The combination of a near-breach readiness score and a stale simulation represents a latent capability gap.

Supplier quality rejection rate of 0.68% is amber (threshold: 0.50%) and the supplier-level data provides clear attribution: Quanta [SUP005] at 1.2% rejection rate and Foxconn [Manufacturing-Assembly] at 0.8% are the primary drivers. Quanta's rate is approaching the 1.0% breach threshold. Given Quanta's concurrent financial distress (health score 61) and inventory cover at breach, quality deterioration at the supplier level may intensify if financial pressure forces cost-cutting in manufacturing process controls.

Warranty claims of 1,842 month-to-date represent a direct EBITDA cost. Per-claim cost data is not available in the current dataset, so aggregate warranty exposure cannot be quantified, but at this volume the monthly cost is material and should be tracked against prior periods to assess trend.

One near-miss event YTD is noted. With recall simulation 8 months stale, the organisation should treat this as a prompt to rerun the exercise before the end of the current quarter.

BCM verdict: No immediate continuity threat from product quality. The risk is forward-looking: deteriorating supplier rejection rates, particularly at Quanta, could translate to downstream quality failures if not arrested, and the recall process is untested against current conditions.

---

O-04 TALENT — BREACH (2 KRIs in breach, 8 amber): ESCALATION REQUIRED

The talent domain has reached an escalation threshold: three flight-risk employees are flagged within R&D, a single critical function. This is not a diffuse attrition problem — it is a concentrated departure risk in the function most directly responsible for product innovation and competitive differentiation. If these individuals exit without structured knowledge transfer, the organisation loses institutional capability that cannot be replaced within the current average fill time of 68 days for critical Engineering roles.

The structural drivers of attrition are clearly identified in the data. Engineering compensation sits 8.2% below market — a meaningful gap in a tight technology labour market. Counter-offer acceptance rate in Engineering is 42%, indicating that when the organisation attempts to retain departing employees, it fails more than half the time. Internal promotion rate of 18% and employee engagement of 67% together suggest limited perceived career progression and low organisational commitment. These are not independent signals — they form a coherent picture of a talent value proposition that is losing competitiveness.

Seven critical roles have been open for more than 60 days (breach threshold: 6), spanning Engineering and IT-Operations based on the attrition data. The IT-Operations attrition rate of 11.8% is near the 12% amber threshold and, if it crosses amber, will directly impair the cyber remediation capacity needed to address the O-02 control failures. This is the most direct operational interconnection between talent and cyber risk in the current dataset.

SVP succession coverage at 72% is amber and declining toward the 70% breach threshold. At the current trajectory, any unplanned SVP departure in an uncovered role creates an immediate leadership gap with no identified successor. Given the scale of operational challenges across O-01 and O-02, senior leadership continuity is a material business continuity factor.

Engineering attrition at 14.2% annualised is amber but not yet at breach (18%). However, at this rate and with compensation 8.2% below market and limited counter-offer effectiveness, the attrition trend is self-reinforcing: departures increase workload on remaining staff, reducing engagement, increasing attrition probability, and further delaying the 7 open critical roles.

BCM verdict: The R&D flight-risk concentration is an immediate escalation item. The talent domain's chronic underinvestment in compensation and progression is the structural driver, and addressing it requires a decision on Engineering compensation benchmarking, targeted retention packages for R&D flight-risk individuals, and a succession plan remediation programme at SVP level. Recovery of open critical roles at 68 days average fill time means the organisation will not be at full critical-role capacity for at least two months even if hiring decisions were made today.

O-02.name: Cyber attack & IT resilience

cyber_supply_response_gap_days.description: Days by which combined MTTD+MTTR cyber response time exceeds available inventory cover. Positive value = production halt guaranteed if cyber incident disrupts supplier comms. Amber: 0 (response just meets inventory); Breach: >0 (response exceeds inventory). Target: ≤ -2 days (2-day safety margin).

O-03.override_justification: Expert override applied 2026-06-07 (EM-08 panel finding). Arithmetic rating: low (l=2, i=5). Override to: high (l=3, i=5). Justification: Likelihood score of 2 is inconsistent with active breach conditions — warranty_claims_mtd at 1,842 (up from 1,612), recall_readiness at 72 (amber, 2 points from breach), recall simulation last run 8 months ago. Upstream supply quality (Quanta rejection rate 1.2%, Foxconn 0.8%) is a confirmed leading driver of downstream field defects. Per O-04 override precedent: forward-looking indicators and breach trajectory take precedence over lagging control effectiveness score. CRO sign-off required next review cycle.

O-04.agent_findings:
OPERATIONAL RISK ASSESSMENT — 2026-05-28

O-01 SUPPLY CHAIN — BREACH (3 KRIs in breach, 1 amber): ESCALATION REQUIRED

The supply chain domain presents the most acute near-term business continuity threat in this assessment cycle. Single-source concentration stands at 62.6% of $12.6B total spend, breaching the 50% threshold and sitting 12.6 percentage points above it. The three single-source suppliers — TSMC ($4.2B, Semiconductors-CPU), Foxconn ($2.1B, Manufacturing-Assembly), and Quanta ($1.6B, ODM-Laptops) — account for $7.9B of spend with no alternative sourcing pathway. The supply chain VaR model places 95th-percentile loss exposure at $2.634B and revenue at risk at $9.1B against a tail disruption scenario.

The inventory position is critical. Minimum cover across the portfolio is 3.9 weeks, breaching the 4-week floor, and the weakest position belongs to Quanta at 3.9 weeks against a 6-week lead time — meaning any Quanta disruption today results in an immediate gap with no buffer. TSMC, the highest-spend single-source supplier, carries 5.2 weeks of cover, but its 18-week lead time means that once stock is consumed, recovery extends to 5 months per the stress model. The organisation currently has no ability to rebuild CPU semiconductor inventory within any commercially meaningful timeframe following a TSMC disruption event.

The Quanta situation triggers the escalation protocol: financial_health score of 61 falls below the 65 distress threshold, and inventory cover sits at exactly 3.9 weeks — below the 4-week breach floor. This is the single-source, low-health, low-inventory combination that represents maximum concentration risk. Quanta's OTD rate of 88.7% is the lowest of the three single-source suppliers, and its rejection rate of 1.2% is the highest. Yangtze Memory [SUP008], a multi-source supplier, also carries a distress score of 55 — the lowest financial health recorded across the supplier base — though multi-source status provides partial mitigation.

Geographic concentration amplifies all of the above. Taiwan accounts for 53.5% of spend (hosting TSMC and Quanta), China for 21.8% (Foxconn), and South Korea for 21.3%. A Taiwan-specific disruption — geopolitical escalation, major seismic event, or TSMC operational failure — would simultaneously affect $4.2B of CPU semiconductor spend and $1.6B of ODM-Laptop spend, covering 46% of total spend in a single geographic event. The stress model quantifies dual-sourcing savings at $382M and inventory buffer savings at $305M — these represent the cost of not acting on known concentration risk, not savings already realised.

BCM verdict: The organisation cannot sustain current production rates beyond 3.9 weeks in a severe supply disruption, and cannot recover within less than 5 months in a Taiwan-concentrated scenario. Revenue exposure is $9.1B. The 24% demand shock probability flagged in the model, carrying a 15% impact, adds a secondary vector to a supply chain already operating with no buffer.

---

O-02 CYBER AND IT — BREACH (6 KRIs in breach, 5 amber): ESCALATION REQUIRED

The cyber domain shows a pattern of compounding control failures rather than a single acute incident. Six metrics are in breach simultaneously, and the combination of a long detection window, slow response, unpatched estate, and zero supplier assessment creates a threat environment where an active adversary could operate undetected and uncontained for an extended period.

MTTD of 11 days breaches the 10-day threshold and means a threat actor has, on average, 11 days of undetected access before any detection signal is raised. When combined with an MTTR of 18 days, the total detection-to-remediation window is approximately 29 days. This is the most consequential control failure in this domain: modern ransomware and data exfiltration campaigns can achieve full compromise objectives within 72 hours of initial access. A 29-day window is operationally indefensible.

Patch compliance at 71% breaches the 80% floor, leaving 29% of the estate unpatched. Eight critical vulnerabilities have been open for more than 30 days, below the breach threshold of 10 but trending in the wrong direction given the detection impairment. Privileged access has not been reviewed for 45 days, creating an uncontrolled lateral movement and insider threat vector. MFA coverage at 87% leaves 13% of access points without a second authentication factor — a meaningful attack surface given the 2.3% phishing attempt rate.

The most structurally concerning finding is supplier cyber resilience assessment at 0%. Not a single supplier has been assessed. Against a backdrop of 4 security incidents month-to-date and a 38% third-party vendor assessment gap, this meets the escalation trigger: zero assessment coverage with active security incidents in the current period. The supply chain interconnection (see O-01 + O-02 flag) is direct: TSMC, Foxconn, and Quanta have unassessed cyber postures while collectively representing $7.9B of single-source spend and serving as production-critical dependencies.

The IT RTO of 6 hours breaches the 4-hour amber threshold. An OMS outage beyond 6 hours would interrupt order processing and fulfilment, with direct revenue recognition impact. The combination of breach-level RTO, unpatched estate, and 29-day detection-to-response window means that a significant incident could breach the RTO objective before the organisation is aware an incident has occurred.

BCM verdict: The current cyber control posture cannot reliably detect, contain, or recover from a significant incident within declared operational tolerances. Revenue continuity through the OMS depends on a system with an unmet recovery objective. The EBITDA cost of a major cyber incident — incident response, regulatory notification, potential ransom, reputational impact — is not quantifiable from available data, but the structural control gaps are severe and unmitigated.

---

O-03 PRODUCT QUALITY — AMBER (3 KRIs amber, 0 breach): NO ESCALATION

The product quality domain is the most stable across this assessment cycle, with no safety incidents, no regulatory notices, and a field failure rate of 0.019% — well within the 0.05% amber threshold and far from the 0.10% breach level. First-pass yield of 97.2% is a healthy signal. There are no grounds for escalation under the zero-tolerance safety incident trigger.

However, two amber signals warrant focused attention and are trending in the wrong direction. Recall readiness score of 72 sits only 2 points above the breach threshold of 70, and the last recall simulation was conducted 8 months ago — an unacceptably long gap for a domain with zero tolerance for safety failures. If a safety event were to emerge without a rehearsed, validated recall process, the organisation's ability to execute a compliant, efficient product withdrawal would be untested. The combination of a near-breach readiness score and a stale simulation represents a latent capability gap.

Supplier quality rejection rate of 0.68% is amber (threshold: 0.50%) and the supplier-level data provides clear attribution: Quanta [SUP005] at 1.2% rejection rate and Foxconn [Manufacturing-Assembly] at 0.8% are the primary drivers. Quanta's rate is approaching the 1.0% breach threshold. Given Quanta's concurrent financial distress (health score 61) and inventory cover at breach, quality deterioration at the supplier level may intensify if financial pressure forces cost-cutting in manufacturing process controls.

Warranty claims of 1,842 month-to-date represent a direct EBITDA cost. Per-claim cost data is not available in the current dataset, so aggregate warranty exposure cannot be quantified, but at this volume the monthly cost is material and should be tracked against prior periods to assess trend.

One near-miss event YTD is noted. With recall simulation 8 months stale, the organisation should treat this as a prompt to rerun the exercise before the end of the current quarter.

BCM verdict: No immediate continuity threat from product quality. The risk is forward-looking: deteriorating supplier rejection rates, particularly at Quanta, could translate to downstream quality failures if not arrested, and the recall process is untested against current conditions.

---

O-04 TALENT — BREACH (2 KRIs in breach, 8 amber): ESCALATION REQUIRED

The talent domain has reached an escalation threshold: three flight-risk employees are flagged within R&D, a single critical function. This is not a diffuse attrition problem — it is a concentrated departure risk in the function most directly responsible for product innovation and competitive differentiation. If these individuals exit without structured knowledge transfer, the organisation loses institutional capability that cannot be replaced within the current average fill time of 68 days for critical Engineering roles.

The structural drivers of attrition are clearly identified in the data. Engineering compensation sits 8.2% below market — a meaningful gap in a tight technology labour market. Counter-offer acceptance rate in Engineering is 42%, indicating that when the organisation attempts to retain departing employees, it fails more than half the time. Internal promotion rate of 18% and employee engagement of 67% together suggest limited perceived career progression and low organisational commitment. These are not independent signals — they form a coherent picture of a talent value proposition that is losing competitiveness.

Seven critical roles have been open for more than 60 days (breach threshold: 6), spanning Engineering and IT-Operations based on the attrition data. The IT-Operations attrition rate of 11.8% is near the 12% amber threshold and, if it crosses amber, will directly impair the cyber remediation capacity needed to address the O-02 control failures. This is the most direct operational interconnection between talent and cyber risk in the current dataset.

SVP succession coverage at 72% is amber and declining toward the 70% breach threshold. At the current trajectory, any unplanned SVP departure in an uncovered role creates an immediate leadership gap with no identified successor. Given the scale of operational challenges across O-01 and O-02, senior leadership continuity is a material business continuity factor.

Engineering attrition at 14.2% annualised is amber but not yet at breach (18%). However, at this rate and with compensation 8.2% below market and limited counter-offer effectiveness, the attrition trend is self-reinforcing: departures increase workload on remaining staff, reducing engagement, increasing attrition probability, and further delaying the 7 open critical roles.

BCM verdict: The R&D flight-risk concentration is an immediate escalation item. The talent domain's chronic underinvestment in compensation and progression is the structural driver, and addressing it requires a decision on Engineering compensation benchmarking, targeted retention packages for R&D flight-risk individuals, and a succession plan remediation programme at SVP level. Recovery of open critical roles at 68 days average fill time means the organisation will not be at full critical-role capacity for at least two months even if hiring decisions were made today.

appetite_thresholds.domain_statement: Low appetite for supply chain disruption and product quality failures. Zero tolerance for major safety incidents. Low tolerance for cyber incidents: MTTD must stay below 5 days; patch compliance must exceed 95%.

---

## 1. DOMAIN STATUS
The strategic risk portfolio is under active pressure, with geopolitical exposure in confirmed breach, AI disruption signals accumulating at amber, and M&A integration performing on target.

---

| Risk | Action |
|---|---|
| **S-01 Geopolitical** | Convene an emergency supply chain review focused on Entity List exposure and Taiwan Strait contingency scenarios, prioritizing alternate sourcing decisions before the next operational cycle. |
| **S-02 AI Disruption** | Commission a competitive gap analysis against the Dell and HP AI-native product announcements to assess whether the current product roadmap requires acceleration or repositioning. |
| **S-03 M&A Integration** | Conduct a focused synergy audit on CloudScale Systems (ACQ001) to identify what is driving the 16-point gap to full target delivery before month-18 close-out obligations. |

---

## 6. ESCALATION

**YES** — S-01 Geopolitical KRI is in confirmed **BREACH** (4 high-severity signals detected). Escalation to executive leadership and the Board Risk Committee is required.

F-01.agent_findings:
## Financial Risk Agent Assessment — Run Date 2026-05-28

### F-01: FX & COMMODITY EXPOSURE

**Scope clarification:** F-01 KRI values are FX-only per CF-04 (2026-06-07). Commodity positions (COM001 DRAM, COM002 NAND) are captured separately for EBITDA covenant stress purposes.

**FX Portfolio Summary (KRI scope):**
Total FX gross exposure: USD 8,600M across four currency pairs (FX001–FX004). Total hedged: USD 3,520M. Total unhedged (FX-only): USD 4,080M. Portfolio average hedge ratio: 46.3%. FX unrealised P&L: USD -26.6M.

**Status: AMBER on two KRIs.**
- Unhedged FX exposure of USD 4,080M exceeds the amber threshold of USD 4,000M. It is USD 920M below the breach threshold of USD 5,000M.
- Average hedge ratio of 46.3% breaches the amber threshold of 60% and sits only 130bps above the breach floor of 45%. This is the more acute metric.
- FX unrealised P&L of USD -26.6M is reported at amber given the combination of below-threshold hedge ratios and negative mark-to-market.

**Position-level detail:**
- FX001 USD_CNY (Revenue, JPMorgan): Gross USD 3,200M, hedge ratio 43.8% — BELOW the 45% breach threshold on a standalone basis. Unhedged: USD 1,800M. This is the single largest unhedged FX position in the portfolio. Maturity 6 months. Unrealised P&L: USD -8.4M.
- FX002 USD_EUR (Revenue, Deutsche Bank): Gross USD 2,800M, hedge ratio 42.9% — also BELOW 45% breach threshold on a standalone basis. Unhedged: USD 1,600M. Maturity 12 months. Unrealised P&L: +USD 7.2M (the only positive P&L position in the portfolio).
- FX003 USD_JPY (Cost, HSBC): Gross USD 980M, hedge ratio 61.2% — above both amber and breach thresholds. Unhedged: USD 380M. Maturity 3 months (nearest maturity — rollover risk). Unrealised P&L: USD -15.6M (largest negative P&L position).
- FX004 USD_KRW (Cost, Citibank): Gross USD 620M, hedge ratio 51.6% — above breach floor but below 60% amber threshold. Unhedged: USD 300M. Maturity 6 months. Unrealised P&L: USD -9.8M.

**Commodity positions (supplementary, not F-01 KRI):**
- COM001 DRAM (Goldman Sachs): Gross USD 890M, hedge 44.9%, unrealised P&L USD -12.3M, maturity 3 months.
- COM002 NAND (JPMorgan): Gross USD 650M, hedge 43.1%, unrealised P&L USD -8.1M, maturity 3 months.
- Combined commodity unrealised P&L: USD -20.4M. Total portfolio (FX + commodity) unrealised P&L: USD -47.0M.

**EBITDA covenant stress split (per hedge model scope):**
- FX positions: USD -26.6M flows through operating income → EBITDA.
- Commodity positions: USD -20.4M flows through COGS → gross profit → EBITDA.
- Total portfolio stress on EBITDA: USD -47.0M against a COV001 headroom of USD 152M.

**Trend: Deteriorating.** Both the aggregate hedge ratio (46.3%) and unhedged exposure (USD 4,080M) breach amber thresholds. FX001 USD_CNY and FX002 USD_EUR have standalone hedge ratios below the 45% breach floor. The FX003 USD_JPY position has a 3-month maturity creating near-term rollover exposure with USD -15.6M unrealised loss. No active programme to increase hedge ratios is evidenced in the data.

**Hedge ratio recommendation (proposed — CFO approval required for execution):** Priority should be assigned to increasing hedge coverage on FX001 USD_CNY (43.8%) and FX002 USD_EUR (42.9%), both of which are below the breach floor on a standalone basis and together account for USD 3,400M of gross exposure.

---

### F-02: REVENUE CONCENTRATION & BAD DEBT

**Top Customer Concentration:**
The single largest customer concentration is 22.1% in Enterprise Tier1. This breaches the amber threshold of 20% and is 2.9 percentage points below the breach threshold of 25%. Status: AMBER.

**Bad Debt Provision:**
Portfolio-level bad debt provision: 1.44%. This breaches both the amber threshold (0.40%) and the breach threshold (0.55%) — and critically, it also constitutes the direct input to the COV006 covenant breach on the Trade-Finance facility (threshold 0.80%). Status: BREACH.

**Segment-level bad debt detail:**
- Enterprise Tier1: provision USD 12.4M — lowest concentration of bad debt risk relative to book size.
- Enterprise Tier2: provision USD 28.6M; overdue 90d USD 18M — elevated relative to Tier1.
- SMB Tier3: provision USD 32.1M; overdue 90d USD 22M — highest overdue 90d balance in absolute terms.
- Government Tier1: provision USD 2.8M; zero overdue 90d — cleanest segment.
- Consumer Tier4: provision USD 18.4M; overdue 90d USD 12M.
- Total overdue 90d: USD 60.0M (provided as context).

**Trend: Deteriorating.** Bad debt at 1.44% is 180bps above the breach threshold of 0.55% and 64bps above the COV006 covenant threshold of 0.80%. The QoQ data shows COV006 current value deteriorated from 1.10% (March test) to 1.44% (June test) — a 34bp worsening — indicating the underlying credit quality trend is adverse rather than static.

---

### F-03: LIQUIDITY & REFINANCING / COVENANT STATUS

**COV006 — CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH (Trade-Finance facility):**
Bad debt provision at 1.44% vs threshold 0.80%. Headroom: -0.64% (in breach by 64bps). This is a confirmed active breach, not a forward probability. Cure deadline: 2026-06-12. Cross-default risk on all facilities if the notification obligation is not met before the 2026-06-30 test date.

Full write-off cure scenario: USD 94.3M bad debt provision write-off flows through P&L, consuming 62% of COV001 EBITDA headroom (USD 152M) and leaving USD ~58M before COV001 breach.
Partial cure scenario (USD 50M write-off): COV001 headroom falls to USD ~102M.

**EXECUTIVE RECOMMENDATION — LENDER NOTIFICATION (COV006):**
(a) BOARD DIRECTIVE REQUIRED: Lender notification is a board-authorised act under credit facility terms and cannot be initiated by management alone.
(b) Written board authorisation is required before the Group Treasurer contacts any lender.
(c) Hard deadline for board resolution: 2026-06-12.
(d) Named joint owners: Group Treasurer AND CFO — CFO co-signature required on all lender communications per facility terms.
(e) Cross-default risk: failure to notify lenders before the 2026-06-30 test date creates cross-default exposure across all facilities including RCF-USD2B, Term-Loan-A, and All-Facilities (COV002, COV003, COV005).

**COV001 — Net Debt / EBITDA (RCF-USD2B): AMBER**
Current ratio: 2.8x. Threshold: 3.0x. Headroom: 0.2x ratio units = USD 152M in EBITDA terms (derived: Net Debt = 2.8 × USD 2.28B = USD 6.384B; binding EBITDA floor = USD 6.384B / 3.0x = USD 2.128B; headroom = USD 2.28B − USD 2.128B = USD 152M). Deteriorated from 2.6x (March) to 2.8x (June). This is the binding covenant — effective headroom is substantially less than the COV005 EBITDA facility threshold suggests.

**COV002 — Interest Coverage (Term-Loan-A): OK**
Current ratio: 4.2x. Threshold: 3.5x. Headroom: 0.7x. Deteriorated from 4.8x (March) to 4.2x (June) — 0.6x compression in one quarter. While no breach, the rate of deterioration warrants monitoring.

**COV003 — Liquidity Headroom (RCF-USD2B): OK**
Current: USD 1.1B. Threshold: USD 0.8B. Headroom: USD 0.3B. Above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger. Compressed from USD 1.2B (March). Does not trigger immediate escalation but adverse trajectory noted.

**COV004 — Debt Maturity Runway (Bond-2027): AMBER**
Current: 8 months. Threshold: 12 months. Headroom: 0 months (in breach of covenant runway metric). Next test: 2026-12-01. Does not trigger the 6-month escalation threshold (runway at 8 months > 6 months), but zero headroom against the 12-month covenant threshold. Deteriorated from 11 months (March) to 8 months (June).

**COV005 — EBITDA (All-Facilities): OK**
Current: USD 2.28B. Threshold: USD 1.80B. Headroom: USD 0.48B at facility threshold. Note: the effective binding constraint is COV001, which implies a floor of USD 2.128B — making the practical headroom USD 152M, not USD 480M.

**Liquidity escalation assessment:** Current liquidity headroom USD 1.1B is above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger. Not escalated on this metric at this time, but the USD 0.2B buffer above the trigger, combined with a deteriorating trend, places this KRI on close watch.

**Trend: Deteriorating.** Every covenant metric with QoQ data (COV001, COV002, COV003, COV004, COV005) has deteriorated between the March and June test dates. COV006 has worsened from 1.10% to 1.44% and remains in active breach. No data evidences an active deleveraging programme.

---

### F-04: FINANCIAL AUDIT FINDINGS

**AUD003 Internal-SOX (Financial Controls):**
Status: Completed. Open high findings: 1. Critical findings: 0. Material weaknesses: 0.

**Status: OK.** One open high-severity finding is below the amber threshold of 2. No material weakness identified — the escalation trigger for material weakness is not activated. The finding count of 1 represents the post-completion state of the AUD003 audit cycle (due 2026-04-30, now completed).

**Trend: Flat.** Single open high finding; no change in material weakness count (0). Insufficient data to identify a directional trend.

---

### CROSS-DOMAIN INTERCONNECTIONS

1. **F-02 → F-03 (mechanical linkage):** The 1.44% bad debt provision (F-02 breach) is the direct and sole driver of the COV006 breach on the Trade-Finance facility (F-03). ## Financial Risk Agent Assessment — Run Date 2026-05-28

### F-01: FX & COMMODITY EXPOSURE

**Scope clarification:** F-01 KRI values are FX-only per CF-04 (2026-06-07). Commodity positions (COM001 DRAM, COM002 NAND) are captured separately for EBITDA covenant stress purposes.

**FX Portfolio Summary (KRI scope):**
Total FX gross exposure: USD 8,600M across four currency pairs (FX001–FX004). Total hedged: USD 3,520M. Total unhedged (FX-only): USD 4,080M. Portfolio average hedge ratio: 46.3%. FX unrealised P&L: USD -26.6M.

**Status: AMBER on two KRIs.**
- Unhedged FX exposure of USD 4,080M exceeds the amber threshold of USD 4,000M. It is USD 920M below the breach threshold of USD 5,000M.
- Average hedge ratio of 46.3% breaches the amber threshold of 60% and sits only 130bps above the breach floor of 45%. This is the more acute metric.
- FX unrealised P&L of USD -26.6M is reported at amber given the combination of below-threshold hedge ratios and negative mark-to-market.

**Position-level detail:**
- FX001 USD_CNY (Revenue, JPMorgan): Gross USD 3,200M, hedge ratio 43.8% — BELOW the 45% breach threshold on a standalone basis. Unhedged: USD 1,800M. This is the single largest unhedged FX position in the portfolio. Maturity 6 months. Unrealised P&L: USD -8.4M.
- FX002 USD_EUR (Revenue, Deutsche Bank): Gross USD 2,800M, hedge ratio 42.9% — also BELOW 45% breach threshold on a standalone basis. Unhedged: USD 1,600M. Maturity 12 months. Unrealised P&L: +USD 7.2M (the only positive P&L position in the portfolio).
- FX003 USD_JPY (Cost, HSBC): Gross USD 980M, hedge ratio 61.2% — above both amber and breach thresholds. Unhedged: USD 380M. Maturity 3 months (nearest maturity — rollover risk). Unrealised P&L: USD -15.6M (largest negative P&L position).
- FX004 USD_KRW (Cost, Citibank): Gross USD 620M, hedge ratio 51.6% — above breach floor but below 60% amber threshold. Unhedged: USD 300M. Maturity 6 months. Unrealised P&L: USD -9.8M.

**Commodity positions (supplementary, not F-01 KRI):**
- COM001 DRAM (Goldman Sachs): Gross USD 890M, hedge 44.9%, unrealised P&L USD -12.3M, maturity 3 months.
- COM002 NAND (JPMorgan): Gross USD 650M, hedge 43.1%, unrealised P&L USD -8.1M, maturity 3 months.
- Combined commodity unrealised P&L: USD -20.4M. Total portfolio (FX + commodity) unrealised P&L: USD -47.0M.

**EBITDA covenant stress split (per hedge model scope):**
- FX positions: USD -26.6M flows through operating income → EBITDA.
- Commodity positions: USD -20.4M flows through COGS → gross profit → EBITDA.
- Total portfolio stress on EBITDA: USD -47.0M against a COV001 headroom of USD 152M.

**Trend: Deteriorating.** Both the aggregate hedge ratio (46.3%) and unhedged exposure (USD 4,080M) breach amber thresholds. FX001 USD_CNY and FX002 USD_EUR have standalone hedge ratios below the 45% breach floor. The FX003 USD_JPY position has a 3-month maturity creating near-term rollover exposure with USD -15.6M unrealised loss. No active programme to increase hedge ratios is evidenced in the data.

**Hedge ratio recommendation (proposed — CFO approval required for execution):** Priority should be assigned to increasing hedge coverage on FX001 USD_CNY (43.8%) and FX002 USD_EUR (42.9%), both of which are below the breach floor on a standalone basis and together account for USD 3,400M of gross exposure.

---

### F-02: REVENUE CONCENTRATION & BAD DEBT

**Top Customer Concentration:**
The single largest customer concentration is 22.1% in Enterprise Tier1. This breaches the amber threshold of 20% and is 2.9 percentage points below the breach threshold of 25%. Status: AMBER.

**Bad Debt Provision:**
Portfolio-level bad debt provision: 1.44%. This breaches both the amber threshold (0.40%) and the breach threshold (0.55%) — and critically, it also constitutes the direct input to the COV006 covenant breach on the Trade-Finance facility (threshold 0.80%). Status: BREACH.

**Segment-level bad debt detail:**
- Enterprise Tier1: provision USD 12.4M — lowest concentration of bad debt risk relative to book size.
- Enterprise Tier2: provision USD 28.6M; overdue 90d USD 18M — elevated relative to Tier1.
- SMB Tier3: provision USD 32.1M; overdue 90d USD 22M — highest overdue 90d balance in absolute terms.
- Government Tier1: provision USD 2.8M; zero overdue 90d — cleanest segment.
- Consumer Tier4: provision USD 18.4M; overdue 90d USD 12M.
- Total overdue 90d: USD 60.0M (provided as context).

**Trend: Deteriorating.** Bad debt at 1.44% is 180bps above the breach threshold of 0.55% and 64bps above the COV006 covenant threshold of 0.80%. The QoQ data shows COV006 current value deteriorated from 1.10% (March test) to 1.44% (June test) — a 34bp worsening — indicating the underlying credit quality trend is adverse rather than static.

---

### F-03: LIQUIDITY & REFINANCING / COVENANT STATUS

**COV006 — CONFIRMED ACTIVE BREACH (Trade-Finance facility):**
Bad debt provision at 1.44% vs threshold 0.80%. Headroom: -0.64% (in breach by 64bps). This is a confirmed active breach, not a forward probability. Cure deadline: 2026-06-12. Cross-default risk on all facilities if the notification obligation is not met before the 2026-06-30 test date.

Full write-off cure scenario: USD 94.3M bad debt provision write-off flows through P&L, consuming 62% of COV001 EBITDA headroom (USD 152M) and leaving USD ~58M before COV001 breach.
Partial cure scenario (USD 50M write-off): COV001 headroom falls to USD ~102M.

**EXECUTIVE RECOMMENDATION — LENDER NOTIFICATION (COV006):**
(a) BOARD DIRECTIVE REQUIRED: Lender notification is a board-authorised act under credit facility terms and cannot be initiated by management alone.
(b) Written board authorisation is required before the Group Treasurer contacts any lender.
(c) Hard deadline for board resolution: 2026-06-12.
(d) Named joint owners: Group Treasurer AND CFO — CFO co-signature required on all lender communications per facility terms.
(e) Cross-default risk: failure to notify lenders before the 2026-06-30 test date creates cross-default exposure across all facilities including RCF-USD2B, Term-Loan-A, and All-Facilities (COV002, COV003, COV005).

**COV001 — Net Debt / EBITDA (RCF-USD2B): AMBER**
Current ratio: 2.8x. Threshold: 3.0x. Headroom: 0.2x ratio units = USD 152M in EBITDA terms (derived: Net Debt = 2.8 × USD 2.28B = USD 6.384B; binding EBITDA floor = USD 6.384B / 3.0x = USD 2.128B; headroom = USD 2.28B − USD 2.128B = USD 152M). Deteriorated from 2.6x (March) to 2.8x (June). This is the binding covenant — effective headroom is substantially less than the COV005 EBITDA facility threshold suggests.

**COV002 — Interest Coverage (Term-Loan-A): OK**
Current ratio: 4.2x. Threshold: 3.5x. Headroom: 0.7x. Deteriorated from 4.8x (March) to 4.2x (June) — 0.6x compression in one quarter. While no breach, the rate of deterioration warrants monitoring.

**COV003 — Liquidity Headroom (RCF-USD2B): OK**
Current: USD 1.1B. Threshold: USD 0.8B. Headroom: USD 0.3B. Above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger. Compressed from USD 1.2B (March). Does not trigger immediate escalation but adverse trajectory noted.

**COV004 — Debt Maturity Runway (Bond-2027): AMBER**
Current: 8 months. Threshold: 12 months. Headroom: 0 months (in breach of covenant runway metric). Next test: 2026-12-01. Does not trigger the 6-month escalation threshold (runway at 8 months > 6 months), but zero headroom against the 12-month covenant threshold. Deteriorated from 11 months (March) to 8 months (June).

**COV005 — EBITDA (All-Facilities): OK**
Current: USD 2.28B. Threshold: USD 1.80B. Headroom: USD 0.48B at facility threshold. Note: the effective binding constraint is COV001, which implies a floor of USD 2.128B — making the practical headroom USD 152M, not USD 480M.

**Liquidity escalation assessment:** Current liquidity headroom USD 1.1B is above the USD 0.9B escalation trigger. Not escalated on this metric at this time, but the USD 0.2B buffer above the trigger, combined with a deteriorating trend, places this KRI on close watch.

**Trend: Deteriorating.** Every covenant metric with QoQ data (COV001, COV002, COV003, COV004, COV005) has deteriorated between the March and June test dates. COV006 has worsened from 1.10% to 1.44% and remains in active breach. No data evidences an active deleveraging programme.

---

### F-04: FINANCIAL AUDIT FINDINGS

**AUD003 Internal-SOX (Financial Controls):**
Status: Completed. Open high findings: 1. Critical findings: 0. Material weaknesses: 0.

**Status: OK.** One open high-severity finding is below the amber threshold of 2. No material weakness identified — the escalation trigger for material weakness is not activated. The finding count of 1 represents the post-completion state of the AUD003 audit cycle (due 2026-04-30, now completed).

**Trend: Flat.** Single open high finding; no change in material weakness count (0). Insufficient data to identify a directional trend.

---

### CROSS-DOMAIN INTERCONNECTIONS

1. **F-02 → F-03 (mechanical linkage):** The 1.44% bad debt provision (F-02 breach) is the direct and sole driver of the COV006 breach on the Trade-Finance facility (F-03). These are not independent risk events. Full cure at USD 94.3M compresses COV001 EBITDA headroom by 62%, creating compound covenant stress.

2. **F-01 → F-03 (EBITDA channel):** The combined unrealised portfolio loss of USD -47.0M (FX: USD -26.6M + commodity: USD -20.4M) represents 30.9% of the USD 152M COV001 EBITDA headroom. If unrealised losses crystallise — particularly on the near-term FX003 USD_JPY (3-month maturity) and commodity positions (both 3-month maturity) — they would flow through EBITDA and narrow the already compressed COV001 headroom.

3. **F-01 + S-01 (geopolitical concentration):** USD_CNY and USD_KRW together represent USD 2,100M of unhedged FX exposure (51.5% of total unhedged FX). Both are currency pairs associated with geopolitical risk. The Strategic Agent S-01 assessment should be cross-referenced to determine whether incremental hedge coverage on these pairs is warranted beyond the standalone financial risk threshold.

4. **F-03 + O-01 (supply chain → EBITDA):** COV001 has compressed from 2.6x to 2.8x in one quarter with only USD 152M EBITDA headroom remaining. Any supply chain disruption reducing revenue or increasing COGS would act additively with the COV006 cure cost to threaten COV001. Operational Agent O-01 supply chain risk data should be cross-referenced for EBITDA impact scenarios.

5. **F-02 + S-03 (M&A and customer concentration):** Enterprise Tier1 top-customer concentration at 22.1% (amber). Any M&A event — acquisition consolidating customers or divestiture reducing the Tier1 book — would shift this KRI materially. Strategic Agent S-03 M&A pipeline data required for forward concentration modelling.

board_summary:
RISK POSTURE

The portfolio records 17 KRI breaches and 18 amber warnings across all four risk domains. With three or more domains simultaneously in breach, the systemic risk flag is active, indicating concurrent domain-level stress requiring board attention.

STRATEGIC: Geopolitical exposure is in confirmed breach, with five signals detected under KRI S-01, triggering formal escalation to the board. Two amber warnings are active, including AI disruption signals accumulating and M&A integration performing on target; the Chief Executive Officer holds accountability.

OPERATIONAL: Ten breaches and seven amber warnings make Operational the most acute risk domain this period. Single-source supply concentration, cyber resilience gaps, talent attrition, and product quality risks are all active; accountability is distributed across the COO, CISO, and CHRO by sub-domain.

FINANCIAL: Four KRI breaches and seven amber warnings are recorded under the Chief Financial Officer's accountability. Bad debt provision at 1.44% is the direct mechanical driver of the COV006 Trade-Finance covenant breach; the full-cure write-off scenario of USD 94.3M would compress COV001 EBITDA headroom from USD 152M to approximately USD 58M. Combined unhedged FX exposure in geopolitically sensitive CNY and KRW pairs stands at USD 2,100M, representing 51.5% of total unhedged exposure of USD 4,080M.

COMPLIANCE: Two KRI breaches are confirmed — AI audit coverage and third-party ABAC coverage — alongside two amber warnings on export screening, under the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel. Incomplete Tier-2 ABAC coverage at 89%, with AUD002 due 2026-06-30, means sanctioned-entity exposure within the unassessed 11% cannot currently be confirmed or excluded. A high-severity GDPR data-processing finding from AUD004 requires joint review with the Operational domain to assess whether a C-02 and O-02 interconnection is triggered.

KEY RISK DRIVERS

The most consequential combination of findings this period is the intersection of financial covenant stress, cyber control failure, and supply chain fragility — three conditions that are individually serious and collectively capable of producing a balance-sheet-level event. The COV006 covenant on the Trade-Finance facility is in confirmed breach, with bad debt provision at 1.44% against a threshold of 0.80%. The full cure cost of USD 94.3 million, if written off, would compress effective COV001 headroom from USD 152 million to approximately USD 58 million — and the company is more likely than not to breach its COV001 bank covenant before year end under current trading conditions. Against this backdrop, the board must authorise lender notification before 2026-06-12: this is a board-authorised act under the facility terms and cannot be delegated to management. Simultaneously, the cyber estate is operating with six KRIs in breach: it takes eleven days on average to detect a security incident against a ten-day threshold, only 71 cents in every dollar of the technology estate is patched against an 80% floor, and privileged access has gone unreviewed for the maximum permitted 45 days. The supply chain position compounds both: inventory cover has fallen to 3.9 weeks against a four-week floor, 62.6% of total spend is concentrated in single-source suppliers, and not one of those suppliers has had its cyber posture assessed — meaning a cyber incident at TSMC, Foxconn, or Quanta would arrive without warning and without a tested response. The reason this combination is more consequential than any individual domain finding is the additive EBITDA channel: a supply chain disruption reducing revenue or increasing costs would act directly on the USD 58 million of effective covenant headroom remaining after the COV006 cure, and a major cyber incident would do the same. The board's first-order concern this period is that these three domains are not independent stress vectors — they are mechanically connected through the EBITDA line.

CROSS-DOMAIN CONNECTIONS

The most direct cross-domain amplification runs from deteriorating credit quality through the financial covenants and into the supply chain's operational resilience. Bad debt provision at 1.44% (F-02 breach) is the sole mechanical driver of the COV006 covenant breach, which in turn triggers cross-default risk across all facilities including the RCF and Term Loan A if lender notification obligations are not met by 30 June 2026. This financial fragility is then directly exposed to supply chain shock: any disruption at a single-source supplier — particularly Quanta, which combines a financial health score of 61, a quality rejection rate approaching the breach threshold, and inventory cover of only 3.9 weeks — would reduce EBITDA and consume the remaining USD 58 million of effective COV001 headroom. The second material connection runs from talent to cyber. Seven critical roles have been open for more than 60 days in Engineering and IT-Operations, and IT-Operations attrition is approaching the amber threshold: this directly constrains the organisation's capacity to remediate the five breached cyber controls, meaning the O-02 control failures are partly structural rather than purely technical. A cyber resilience failure in this understaffed environment — with a 29-day combined detection-to-remediation window — could simultaneously interrupt order management system availability, impair revenue recognition, and, if it affected a single-source supplier with no assessed cyber posture, trigger a supply disruption that further compresses covenant headroom. The compliance dimension adds a further layer: 11% of third-party suppliers remain unassessed under anti-bribery frameworks, three denied-party matches are pending resolution, and two export licences expire within 30 days. Given that geopolitical signal count has reached five against a threshold of three — with signals spanning US Entity List expansion, Taiwan Strait instability, and OFAC designations of Russian technology intermediaries — the unassessed supplier population carries sanctions exposure that cannot currently be confirmed or excluded. The EU AI Act enforcement deadline is 54 days away with audit coverage at 78% against a 90% breach threshold, and the penalty exposure for non-compliance reaches 3% of global annual turnover: this is not a compliance programme issue in isolation but a financial exposure that management must quantify alongside the covenant stress already on the balance sheet.

QUARTER-ON-QUARTER MOVEMENT

Aggregate residual risk has risen materially from the prior period. Fifteen of the twenty KRIs tracked on a comparable basis show a deteriorating trend direction, against three improving and two stable. The dominant cause is inherent risk growth: the underlying threat environment has expanded regardless of what controls have done. Four KRIs moved into breach for the first time this period — MTTD rising from nine to eleven days, critical open roles increasing from five to seven, inventory cover falling from 4.3 to 3.9 weeks, and geopolitical signal count rising from two to five. Each of these represents a genuine expansion of underlying exposure rather than a failure to maintain an existing defence. Control weakening is also present — patch compliance has continued to deteriorate from 78% to 71% while already in breach, and the supplier cyber resilience assessment rate has fallen from 15% to 0% — but these are secondary to the inherent risk growth driving new breaches. The single KRI whose reversal would reduce residual risk most this period is mean time to detect a security incident. Bringing MTTD back below the ten-day threshold would reduce the window available to any active threat actor, directly enable faster containment before revenue systems are affected, and restore the foundational detection capability on which all other cyber remediation depends. The board's primary decision point is therefore whether the CISO has the staffing and investment authority needed to close this detection gap within the current quarter.

RISK COMMITTEE RECOMMENDED ACTIONS:
  • Direct the General Counsel and CFO — by the action deadline of 2026-06-12 — to report to the Committee on the steps being taken to resolve the cross-default risk (F-03 binary breach) triggered by the COV006 covenant breach, including confirmation of any cure mechanism, lender notification obligations, and the financial impact of the USD 94.3 million full-cure write-off on effective COV001 headroom, which stands at USD 57.7 million. The Committee must be informed of any further deterioration before that date.
  • Require the CFO to present the definitive covenant breach probability by 2026-06-20, replacing the current PROVISIONAL figure of 50.5% (more likely than not at current trajectory), and direct that no new debt, material capital commitment, or discretionary spending above a threshold set by the CFO be approved until the definitive figure is available and headroom has been confirmed. The Committee notes that bad debt provision at 1.44% — nearly three times the 0.55% threshold and deteriorating — is the mechanical driver of the COV006 breach and of EBITDA compression.
  • Direct the CISO to deliver within 30 days a time-bound remediation plan covering all five breached cyber KRIs — MTTD at 11 days, patch compliance at 71%, MFA coverage at 87%, IT recovery time at threshold, and privileged access unreviewed for 45 days — and to initiate supplier cyber resilience assessments across all suppliers representing single-source spend, with the aim of reaching at least the 50% assessment threshold before the next Committee meeting. The Committee notes that the current 0% assessment rate leaves USD 7.9 billion of single-source supplier spend with no assessed cyber posture, directly amplifying supply chain disruption risk given inventory cover below the 4.0-week threshold.
  • Approve a directed instruction to the COO and VP Supply Chain to present at the next Committee meeting a specific plan to: (a) restore inventory cover above 4.0 weeks, (b) reduce single-source concentration below 50%, and (c) address the geographic concentration at 75.3% against a 75% threshold. The Committee must be informed of the financial cost and timeline of any buffer-stock or dual-sourcing programme, and the plan must address the identified risk that a financial or operational failure at a named single-source supplier carrying a 1.2% quality rejection rate and a financial health score of 61 would simultaneously disrupt supply and product quality.
  • Require the Chief Compliance Officer and General Counsel to confirm to the Committee, before the AUD002 deadline of 2026-06-30, that third-party ABAC coverage has reached the 95% threshold — currently at 89% — and that the unassessed 11% of third parties has been screened against restricted-party and denied-party lists, given that two denied-party matches and three pending matches are already identified. Separately, direct the Chief Compliance Officer to convene a joint review with the CISO of the high-severity finding from the AUD004 GDPR data-processing audit to determine whether it implicates systems subject to O-02 cyber controls.

cov001_board_narrative.text: IMPORTANT — COV001 AMBER RATIO VS FORWARD BREACH PROBABILITY: The Net Debt to EBITDA ratio currently reads 2.8× (amber — below the 3.0× breach ceiling). This ratio reflects the position as at the Q2 reporting date and is correct. However, the EBITDA Stress Monte Carlo separately estimates a 50.5% probability that this ratio will exceed 3.0× (model_params authoritative MC value using effective headroom USD 57.7M — 2026-06-09; original figure 69.4% was a calculation error corrected 2026-06-09) by the 2026-06-30 test date — a breach-status forward indicator. These two signals are not contradictory: the ratio measures where the organisation is today; the Monte Carlo measures where it is likely to be in 21 days under plausible EBITDA scenarios. Both signals must be read together. The amber ratio does NOT indicate the covenant position is under control — the forward probability of 50.5% means more likely than not that a COV001 breach will occur at the next test date. The board should treat this as a breach-trajectory situation requiring the same urgency as an active breach. A deterministic scenario in which the COV006 provision charge of USD 94.3M (corrected; prior USD 365M was a calculation error) recognised in full would reduce EBITDA to approximately USD 2,185.7M — USD 57.7M above the COV001 implied floor of USD 2,128M — meaning the cure alone does not deterministically breach COV001, but the Monte Carlo shows P(breach) = 50.5% under plausible EBITDA scenarios at test date.
